sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Persistent pain care in FIFOEMS should validate the patient's experience while screening for change that suggests something new, dangerous, or functionally destabilising.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Ongoing pain with episodic flares
- Reduced function, poor sleep, distress, or fear of movement
- Variable tolerance and boom-bust activity pattern
- Prior imaging or diagnoses that do not clearly explain current disability

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- A persistent pain history does not protect the patient from new pathology
- Flare drivers may include load spike, stress, poor sleep, travel, illness, or work conflict
- Escalate for new neurological deficit, infection signs, unexplained weight loss, fracture concern, or major mood deterioration

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen whether this flare is typical or clearly different from baseline
- Use reassurance, pacing, symptom relief, and load reduction within scope
- Escalate if the story or examination suggests a new serious process

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Identify flare triggers and build a plan for graded activity recovery, sleep, pacing, and confidence restoration
- Coordinate with workplace, medical, and mental health supports when the pain presentation is entangled with broader recovery barriers
- Avoid repeated passive treatment-only cycles without a functional plan

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Persistent pain management is strongest when it gives the patient a usable plan for the next 48 hours, not just a label
